story:
Father Michael Dravin, a former military chaplain, has spent three years breathing life back into Saint Neot’s, a small, aging parish named for the patron saint of fish. He learns the saint’s stories, turns Lenten fish fries into neighborhood events, digs a pond in back, and opens the doors to youth groups. The church becomes a net, drawing people from miles around—a quiet place in a city growing colder and harder. On the eve of Saint Neot’s feast day—hours before the second annual Neot’s Catch—Father Michael follows his ritual: rosary, prayers, candles, a word with Mrs. Rosen, and a few minutes by the pond.

This evening, gunshots shatter the stillness. He freezes long enough to count them, to listen for screams or running feet, and then forces his fingers back to the rosary, prayer the only steady thing he has to offer the dark. Footsteps cross the stone floor and a cold muzzle presses to his head. A trembling voice orders him not to move. The stranger, claiming he has shot three people, demands to know if anyone else is coming. Father Michael answers calmly and asks to sit; an old war injury makes kneeling hurt. The gunman, raw with anger, allows it. He gives his name: Zale.

Trying to keep him talking, Father Michael begins to tell the legend of Saint Neot, the “patron saint of fish.” At the word, Zale explodes, fury and grief boiling over. Zale asks why the priest cares. Father Michael admits he doesn’t want to die, but he also wants to listen. “Part of my calling is to be a fisher of men.”

Zale’s story spills out. Ten years earlier, on the last morning of a family vacation, he raced his older brother Delmar out to a buoy. Delmar, usually faster, slowed and let Zale surge ahead. Then a scream split the air. A great white shark took Delmar under in a plume of red water. The body was never recovered. Zale’s mother blamed him for the race; his father later killed himself with the pistol now in Zale’s hand. Lost and desperate, with a mother passed out and rent due, Zale says he’s been using the gun to collect debts and fear, insisting people “needed it.” Father Michael refuses the lie. Some things are beyond control—like a shark in deep water—but pulling a trigger is not. He invites Zale to the pond, not by reaching for him, only by rising slowly and trusting grief to follow where force would fail.

By the water, Father Michael recounts Saint Neot’s miracle: three fish kept by God in a pond; one may be eaten each day and the number never fails. When Neot’s servant panicked and cooked two fish, Neot ordered them returned; at the touch of water, life came back. The next day, one fish was taken, and Neot was healed. The priest says he hears God’s quiet voice in the ripple of fins. He suggests a harder truth: perhaps Delmar slowed because he had seen the shark and was trying to shield his little brother. Zale has never considered it. Father Michael does not call it certainty, only mercy—a possibility large enough to live inside. The thought softens him.

Police reach the courtyard, weapons drawn. Zale whips around. Father Michael steps between him and the officers. He cannot stop a bullet, but he can stop panic from choosing one. He will not excuse what Zale has done, but he will not abandon him. He pleads for surrender: don’t throw away the miracle that is your life. After a breath held by heaven, Zale kneels and drops the gun. He is arrested, and the priest, now weeping, watches him go.

Years later, during the twelfth Neot’s Catch, Father Michael spots a familiar face, older and steadier. Zale has brought a silver fish cleared for the pond, bright as a coin in his hands. With a small crowd of children watching, he releases it into the water. When a girl asks its name, he smiles gently. “Delmar.”
